did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Back Propagated Artificial Neural Network (BP-ANN) for the classification of skin cancer (specifically distinguishing between benign and melanoma/malignant cases). The method involves preprocessing dermoscopy images, segmentation using Otsu's method, feature extraction using the Spatially Gray Level Dependency Matrix (SGLD), and finally classification using the trained BP-ANN model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including a 'Suggested Method' section detailing a specific approach using Back Propagated Artificial Neural Networks (BP-ANN), preprocessing steps, segmentation, and feature extraction (SGLD). It describes a specific dataset (ISIC Archive with 2000 images), training/testing procedures, and reports experimental results and conclusions, which distinguishes it from a narrative or systematic review despite the title containing 'Systematic Study'.

title: A Systematic Study And Approach On Detection Of Classification Of Skin Cancer Using Back Propagated Artificial Neural Networks
journal: Turkish Journal of Computer and Mathematics Education
brief_summary: This study proposes a computational methodology for the detection and classification of skin cancer, specifically distinguishing between benign and malignant (melanoma) cases, using dermoscopy images. The approach utilizes the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive dataset, comprising 1000 benign and 1000 malignant images. The pipeline involves preprocessing with sharpening and smoothing filters to remove noise and hair artifacts, followed by Otsu's method for region of interest segmentation. Features are extracted using the Spatially Gray Level Dependency Matrix (SGLD) and classified using a Back Propagated Artificial Neural Network (BP-ANN). The authors conclude that this multi-layer, error-resilient BP-ANN model effectively addresses challenges in precise segmentation and feature extraction for accurate skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper explicitly states the classification of 'Benign and Melanoma skin cancers'. The dataset used (ISIC Archive) comprises 1000 malignant images, which are identified as melanoma in the context of the study's focus on the deadliest skin cancer.

The paper classifies 'Benign and Melanoma skin cancers'. The dataset used (ISIC Archive) comprises 1000 benign images. The text also references classifying 'non-melanoma' lesions in the literature survey section.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant (Melanoma) skin cancer status
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is the 'classification of Benign and Melanoma skin cancers' using a Back Propagated Artificial Neural Network (BP-ANN). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories: normal, benign, or malignant. The abstract and introduction confirm the task is distinguishing between benign and malignant (melanoma) cases based on dermoscopy images.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive
datasets_used.description: A collection of quality-controlled dermoscopic images used as the primary database for the study.

use.training: All 2000 images (1000 benign and 1000 malignant) were used to train the BP-ANN network model with SGLD features.
use.testing: A random unknown test sample from the dataset was applied to the system for detection and classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports performance metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity but does not provide statistical significance analysis.

ISIC Archive.metric: accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, PSNR, MSE
ISIC Archive.notes: The paper states that the suggested research work can be 'effectively used for the classification of Benign and Melanoma skin cancers' and that the BP-ANN model achieves 'maximum accuracy'. However, specific quantitative numerical results (e.g., exact percentage values for accuracy, sensitivity, etc.) for the proposed BP-ANN model on the ISIC dataset are not reported in the provided text chunks. The text mentions that 'experimental results' were analyzed in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values, but does not list the final numbers. It also mentions that a 'random unknown test sample is applied to the system for detection and classification', but does not provide the outcome metrics for this specific test.

architecture_details.structure: Sequence of three alternating Convolutional 2D layers and Maxpooling2D layers, followed by three fully connected layers

convolutional_layers.filters: 5 (described as 256*256 pixel filters in text, likely error for filter count or size)

architecture_details.hidden_layers_description: Two levels of hidden layer hold normality and abnormalities; second stage holds benign and malignant cancer weights
architecture_details.feature_input: Spatially Gray Level Dependency Matrix (SGLD) features

classification_or_output_head.classification_method: Feature matching using Euclidean distance manner based on maximum feature matching criteria
classification_or_output_head.mapping: Labels mapped in output layer based on hidden layer weight distributions

preprocessing_techniques: De-noising (to remove salt and pepper, Gaussian, speckle, and Poisson noise)
Removal of unwanted background parts, labels, tape, artifacts, and pectoral muscle
conclusions: The suggested research work using a Back Propagated Artificial Neural Network (BP-ANN) with Spatially Gray Level Dependency Matrix (SGLD) features can be effectively used for the classification of Benign and Melanoma skin cancers.
The BP-ANN model achieves maximum accuracy for the classification of skin cancer.
The multi-layer, error-resilient BP-ANN model effectively addresses challenges in precise segmentation and feature extraction for accurate skin cancer classification.
The proposed system utilizes the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive dataset, comprising 1000 benign and 1000 malignant images, for training the network model.
important_remarks: The paper claims to use a Back Propagated Artificial Neural Network (BP-ANN) but describes a hybrid architecture that includes Convolutional 2D layers, MaxPooling, and fully connected layers, which is structurally a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), not a traditional BP-ANN.

The reported architecture contains contradictory or erroneous details, such as a second convolutional layer with '5 filters' described as '256*256 pixel filters' and a total parameter count of '60 million' for a model described as having only 8 layers.

Specific quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) for the proposed model are not reported in the text; the authors only make qualitative claims of 'maximum accuracy' and 'effectively used'.

The dataset split is unclear: while 2000 images from the ISIC Archive are mentioned for training, the testing procedure is vaguely described as applying a 'random unknown test sample' without specifying the test set size or composition.
There is a lack of external validation; the study relies solely on the ISIC Archive dataset without mentioning cross-validation or independent test sets.

The conclusion section contains contradictory statements regarding preprocessing: one bullet point states standard filters 'failed to remove the hair artifacts', while the subsequent paragraph claims the filtering methods 'can effectively removes the hair'.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the claimed superiority of the method.

The study focuses only on Benign vs. Malignant (Melanoma) classification despite the introduction listing other skin cancer types (e.g., Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratoses), limiting the generalizability of the findings.
